title: Tragopogon buphthalmoides
taxonomy: Tragopogon buphthalmoides belongs to the family Asteraceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the sunflower or daisy family. Within this family, Tragopogon buphthalmoides is placed in the genus Tragopogon, which includes various species of salsify or goatsbeard. The genus Tragopogon is further divided into several sections, with Tragopogon buphthalmoides being a part of the section Buphthalmoides. This classification is based on the plant's morphology, genetics, and evolutionary relationships with other species. Tragopogon buphthalmoides is native to certain regions and has been studied by botanists to understand its classification and evolutionary history.
uses: Tragopogon buphthalmoides, a perennial plant species, has been traditionally used in various folk remedies for its medicinal properties. The roots and flowers of the plant are reported to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making it useful in treating various skin conditions and infections. In traditional medicine, it has been employed to treat issues such as eczema, acne, and wounds. However, further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using T. buphthalmoides for medicinal purposes. It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using this plant for therapeutic purposes.
properties: Tragopogon buphthalmoides, also known as the small golden-scales or false dandelion, is a plant species with a rich history of traditional use in folk medicine. Its therapeutic properties are attributed to the presence of bioactive compounds such as flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are known for their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. Studies have also suggested that extracts from Tragopogon buphthalmoides may have antiviral and antibacterial properties, making it a potential candidate for the development of new therapeutic agents. Further research is needed to fully elucidate the therapeutic potential of this plant species.
constituents: Tragopogon buphthalmoides contains various active constituents, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids. These compounds contribute to its medicinal properties. Research has identified the presence of kaempferol, a flavonoid, as one of the key active constituents in this plant. The specific combination and concentration of these constituents can vary depending on factors such as location and growing conditions. Further studies are needed to fully understand the range of active constituents in Tragopogon buphthalmoides.

side_effects: Tragopogon buphthalmoides is a plant species, but there is limited information available on its side effects. It is primarily known for its ornamental value and is used as a food source in some cultures. However, its toxicity profile is not well-documented, and it may cause adverse reactions if ingested. intro: Tragopogon buphthalmoides, also known as the golden salsify or yellow salsify, is a perennial plant species within the dandelion family. It has been traditionally used in herbal medicine due to its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, helping to alleviate symptoms of digestive issues, fever, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Tragopogon buphthalmoides include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include tinctures, infusions, and decoctions, often used to support digestive health and immune function.
